evals.prompt: I want to add a Cursor harness adapter to Memory Lane but I've never touched the adapters package. Do a blindspot pass.
evals.expected_output: A blindspot-pass report grounded in the actual codebase, listing unknown unknowns and ending with concrete prompting advice.

assertions.text: Establishes the user's starting point (asks for it, or explicitly uses the experience level stated in the prompt) before diagnosing
Findings are grounded in the real codebase (cites actual files/types/conventions), not generic advice
Routes to the blindspot-pass technique only; does not run interviews, brainstorms, or implementation plans in the same response
Includes 'how to prompt better' guidance derived from the findings

evals.prompt: I want a landing page hero for my agency site but I'll know it when I see it.
evals.expected_output: Routes to brainstorm/prototype: one disposable artifact with ~4 genuinely divergent directions to react to.

assertions.text: Classifies as unknown-knowns and routes to brainstorms & prototypes, not an interview about preferences the user said they cannot articulate
Produces or proposes a single disposable artifact (e.g., one HTML mock) with multiple genuinely different directions, not one polished implementation
Does not wire up real infrastructure or production code

evals.prompt: Interview me about anything ambiguous in this spec before we build it.
evals.expected_output: An interview conducted one question at a time, prioritized by architectural impact.

assertions.text: Asks exactly one question, then waits for the answer (does not batch a questionnaire)
First question targets a decision that would change the architecture or approach, not cosmetic details

evals.prompt: We're ready to build. Write the implementation plan for the feature we discussed.
evals.expected_output: A decisions-first plan: data models, type interfaces, and user-facing behavior at top; mechanical refactoring at bottom marked as not needing review.

assertions.text: Plan orders content by likelihood the user will want to change it: data model changes, type interfaces, and user-facing behavior appear before mechanical refactoring
Mechanical/low-risk sections are explicitly marked as not needing close review
Recommends starting a fresh session/context with the approved plan and artifacts

evals.prompt: Start implementing the approved plan now. Track anything that forces you off-plan.
evals.expected_output: Implementation proceeds with a running implementation-notes.md containing assumptions and a Deviations section.

assertions.text: Creates or maintains an implementation-notes.md (or .html) file during the work
Notes file contains a 'Deviations' section logging departures from the plan
When hitting an edge case, picks the conservative option, logs it, and continues rather than stopping

evals.prompt: Claude Code just did a big refactor of the lifecycle package. Quiz me before I merge.
evals.expected_output: A report (context, intuition, what changed and why) ending in a 3-7 question quiz with a merge gate.

assertions.text: Output ends with a quiz of 3-7 questions
Questions prioritize behavior that depends on existing code paths over surface diff content
States the merge gate: don't merge until the quiz is passed cleanly

evals.prompt: Package this change so I can get buy-in from the team in Slack.
evals.expected_output: A single pitch/explainer artifact combining spec, prototype, and implementation notes, leading with a demo.

assertions.text: Produces one consolidated artifact (not scattered pieces) combining spec, prototype, and implementation notes where available
Leads with the demo or most visual element
Addresses reviewer unknowns up front (resolves the unknowns a fresh reviewer would start with)

evals.prompt: Fix this typo in the README: 'recieve' should be 'receive'.
evals.expected_output: Skill does not engage its process; the fix is made directly.

assertions.text: Does not run any knock-knock technique (no blindspot pass, interview, or plan) for a trivial task
